Title: Old Gods, New Enigmas: Marx's Lost Theory
Author: Mike  Davis
My Review:
Definitely one of the hardest books I’ve read. I imagine these are the types of books you read if you majored in something besides film.

It wasn’t until reading other Good Reads reviews that I even began to understand what I was reading which was three essentially separate essays. In the end I think my favorite was the first part, which was also the densest to read, a history of workers revolutions from the past few hundred years. Cool beans!

The final essay is an argument with himself about whether humanity can avoid eco-annihilation and unfortunately his optimistic side did not convince me to have any hope....

Title: Mao's China and After: A History of the People's Republic
Author: Maurice J. Meisner
My Review:
I feel like I now know more about the philosophies and people that created early China than I do with early America...

Definitely written by someone with an appreciation and sympathy for communist ideas, the author also makes very clears the missteps, ironies and destruction caused by the contradiction that is modern China.

When describing the Mao era, he would take great pains to describe the changes in the seeming minutia of agricultural outputs and grain taxes but as he approached present day and entered the Deng period he was able to condense more learning in a much easier read.

It is curious that the event described in the most physical details (as opposed to political ramifications) is Deng’s Beijing suppression in 1989 which killed around 8,000 where many of the (more numerous) deaths caused by Mao’s policies are brushed over and ideologically rationalized. Still, I get it tho.

Title: How Europe Underdeveloped Africa
Author: Walter Rodney
My Review:
It's always a positive when the author of the book you are reading was later assassinated for his beliefs and actions.

That being said, I hoped this book would give me a better historical understanding of how modern African states came to be. Written in 1965, during the heated early post-colonial days, this book assumes the reader obviously knows about the scramble for Africa and various other important events. This book assumed wrong, but I was still able to pick up a lot through context clues.

What this book does an amazing job explaining is how 'the west' developed at the direct expense of Africa. Exhaustively going through various reasons, Rodney shows the material ways the colonizers stunted and reversed African development. I also loved in the end how he showed the dialectical nature of colonialism created a small educated class of ideally subservient Africans who would later end up breaking their hold. (But obvi not completely breaking it, I need to know whats been up with Africa for the past 60 years...)

I personally did not need the abundance of details regarding specific banks, tribes, kingdoms and companies involved. But I can't complain...

P.S. I also enjoyed the section jumping around Africa pre-colonialism and giving little tastes of what life was like through the continent from. 1300-1600.

P.P.S. Walter Rodney peppers some funny lines in there, thank you!

Title: Exiting the Vampire Castle
Author: Mark Fisher
My Review:
From lame online reactions to a Russell Brand stand up set, Fisher was able to see the future...

Some understand this all intrinsically now, having been inundated with it for almost half a decade, but many still don't. Others are probably blissfully unaware... and others see it happening but deny it to themselves, lest they be bad people!

Sometimes I want to be Van Helsing the way these vampires be sucking....

Title: What Is to Be Done?
Author: Vladimir Lenin
My Review:
Lenin is a sassy man. Shout out Revolutions Podcast because without that this would have made no sense at all. Instead it made some sense!

This manifesto is a series of hyper-specific responses to specific criticisms and organizations in Russia at the beginning of the 20th century. But beneath the funny call outs and bitter attacks, he lays out some universal ideas that seem very relevant to today.

I love angry people who think they are right and everyone else is wrong....

Title: A Brief History of Neoliberalism
Author: David Harvey
My Review:
We live in a world of increasingly heightened contradictions. The further ones actions are from their words, the more this becomes demystified.

The greatest contradiction is how a book so good, so illuminating can also be so boring. How can the secrets of the modern condition be revealed to me and at the same time make me never want to learn or read again?

Read the first and last 50 pages and you’ll get all you need.

Glad I know what neoliberalism is now, I also kind of know what Keynesian means now. I also learned a few other things! What I did not learn is who is the second from the right face on the cover? The mysterious man of this Neoliberal Mount Rushmore...

Like “Mao’s China and After” I’m left at the end of this book trying to fill the gaps between when it was written and now. I’m sure I can Google his takes on this, maybe I will...

Thanks @jeff for the recc, next time please make it a more interesting one. My good reads 2021 goals has been a dealt a near fatal blow due to this slog!

Title: Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?
Author: Mark Fisher
My Review:
Great book + some good movies analysis

One select quote :

In a seeming irony, the media class’s refusal to be paternalistic has not produced a bottom-up culture of breathtaking diversity, but one that is increasingly infantilized. By contrast, it is paternalistic cultures that treat audiences as adults, assuming that they can cope with cultural products that are complex and intellectually demanding. The reason that focus groups and capitalist feedback systems fail, even when they generate commodities that are immensely popular, is that people do not know what they want. This is not only because people’s desire is already present but concealed from them (although this is often the case). Rather, the most powerful forms of desire are precisely cravings for the strange, the unexpected, the weird. These can only be supplied by artists and media professionals who are prepared to give people something different from that which already satisfies them; by those, that is to say, prepared to take a certain kind of risk.
